patient.chief_complaint: My kid keeps getting these fevers that come out of nowhere.

volunteer: Fevers that come and go every few weeks, even when there’s no obvious cold
Gets really tired and pale a lot of the time
Not growing or keeping up with other kids in speech and learning
if_asked: During the fever episodes, they complain of body aches and just want to lie down
Sometimes gets mouth sores around the time of the fevers
Gets infections more than other kids (ear infections and bad colds that linger)
Bruises easily and sometimes has nosebleeds
Poor appetite, especially during fever weeks
hidden: Breathes fast or gets winded easily when playing compared with other kids
Occasional yellowish tint to the eyes after fever episodes
Dark stools at times (family thought it was from vitamins/foods)
Needs more sleep than other kids and seems wiped out the day after a fever breaks
resistant: Family has delayed some vaccines and medical visits because they feel judged and worry about being blamed
misleading: Knee and ankle pains during fever episodes, and the parent wonders if it’s ‘juvenile arthritis’
The fevers seem to happen in a pattern, and the parent read online about ‘cyclic blood counts’ causing periodic illness
noise: Occasional headaches after long screen time
Dry skin in winter
Picky eating (prefers carbs)

general_concerns: Thinks the periodic fevers mean he is 'always contagious' and should avoid other kids.
Believes anemia is caused mainly by not eating enough spinach or red meat and that diet alone can fix it.
Thinks getting vaccines will 'overload' his immune system and make infections worse.
Believes antibiotics should be used whenever he has a fever, even if there are no signs of bacterial infection.
Thinks developmental delays are caused by something he did wrong or by not trying hard enough at school.
economic_concerns: Family worries that frequent lab tests and specialist visits are optional and can be skipped when he seems well.
Parent believes stretching medications (taking less to make them last longer) is safe if symptoms are mild.
Concern that missing work for medical appointments will risk job security, so they prefer fewer follow-ups.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 8-year-old son. Be cooperative but vague unless asked. Start with: “He keeps getting fevers that come out of nowhere.” If the clinician asks open questions, volunteer: fevers every few weeks without a clear cold, he’s often tired and pale, and he’s not keeping up with other kids in speech/learning. If asked about fever episodes, add: body aches, wants to lie down, poor appetite, sometimes mouth sores, frequent ear infections/lingering colds, easy bruising and occasional nosebleeds. Do not mention unless specifically asked: he gets winded/fast breathing with play, occasional yellowish eyes after fevers, sometimes dark stools, and he’s wiped out the day after a fever breaks. Bring up your worries/misconceptions: “Could this be juvenile arthritis?” and “I read about cyclic blood counts causing periodic illness.” Be resistant about vaccines/visits: say you’ve delayed some because you feel judged. Express cost/job concerns and preference for fewer labs/follow-ups; say you sometimes stretch medicines. If the clinician shows empathy, soften and share more details.

diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
differentials: juvenile arthritis due to defect in LACC1

correct_diagnosis.primary: congenital sideroblastic anemia-B-cell immunodeficiency-periodic fever-developmental delay syndrome
acceptable_alternatives: sideroblastic anemia with immunodeficiency and autoinflammation
required_evidence: CBC pattern consistent with anemia (low hemoglobin/hematocrit) with microcytosis (low MCV) and elevated RDW
inappropriately low reticulocyte count for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative anemia)
clinical history consistent with syndromic features: recurrent/periodic fevers, B-cell immunodeficiency (recurrent infections), and developmental delay
exclusion of common alternative explanations for microcytic anemia (e.g., dietary iron deficiency) and consideration of inherited sideroblastic anemia when persistent and unexplained

expected_lab_results.RBC count: Low to normal (often relatively preserved compared with degree of anemia in microcytosis)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Low or inappropriately normal (not appropriately elevated for anemia)
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated during febrile episodes; otherwise may be normal

medications: pyridoxine (vitamin B6) 50 mg PO daily
folic acid 1 mg PO daily
acetaminophen 15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever (max 60 mg/kg/day or 3 g/day, whichever is lower)
intravenous immune globulin (IVIG) 400-600 mg/kg IV every 3-4 weeks (if documented clinically significant B-cell immunodeficiency with recurrent/severe infections)
non_pharmacological: refer to hematology for confirmation of sideroblastic anemia subtype and transfusion strategy; consider bone marrow evaluation/genetic confirmation if not already established
refer to immunology for immune workup and infection-prevention plan (vaccination review; avoid live vaccines if significant immunodeficiency)
transfuse packed RBCs as clinically indicated for symptomatic anemia or severe anemia per institutional thresholds
monitor for iron overload if transfusion-dependent; arrange ferritin and iron studies when available and consider chelation per hematology
fever plan: prompt evaluation for infection during febrile episodes given immunodeficiency; obtain cultures and start empiric antibiotics when clinically indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology and immunology follow-up within 2-4 weeks; sooner (same day/within 24 hours) for any fever with toxicity, poor perfusion, respiratory distress, or signs of serious infection.

must_include: the condition is an inherited syndrome affecting red blood cell production (sideroblastic anemia) and immune function (B-cell immunodeficiency)
why anemia can cause fatigue/pallor and why fevers/infections may be more frequent
the need to confirm/monitor with specialist care and periodic blood tests
goal of pyridoxine/folate is to support red blood cell production; response is monitored on CBC/reticulocytes
when transfusions are needed and what symptoms should trigger urgent care
if IVIG is used: purpose (infection prevention), schedule, and common side effects (headache, infusion reactions)
review the amoxicillin-clavulanate allergy (urticarial rash) and avoid prescribing it; discuss alternative antibiotics if needed
return precautions for fever given immunodeficiency (seek care promptly, especially if ill-appearing)
discuss long-term monitoring needs (anemia severity, infections, potential iron overload if transfused)
infection prevention steps (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts when possible, staying up to date on recommended vaccines per immunology)
do not start iron supplements unless instructed (microcytosis is not automatically iron deficiency in this syndrome)
keep a fever/anemia symptom diary and bring it to follow-up visits

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 56-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start with: “I’ve had this cough that just won’t go away, and I get out of breath way easier than I used to.” Early in the visit, volunteer: dry cough for a few weeks, shortness of breath with stairs/chores, more fatigue, and low-grade evening fevers. If asked about other symptoms, share: occasional chest tightness with deep breaths, a few pounds of unintentional weight loss from low appetite, night sweats a couple times/week, and that your doctor mentioned a “hollow/blowing” sound in one lung area. Do not volunteer that you get winded just talking/carrying groceries, that you’ve avoided exercise, or that you take extra breaks at work unless specifically asked about daily activities/limitations. Withhold vaping unless directly asked about smoking/vaping/substances; then admit you vape on and off and feel a bit embarrassed. Mention misconceptions: you tried an old inhaler with little help, took leftover antibiotics for 2 days thinking infection, and wonder if reflux/heartburn is causing cough. Ask about cost: whether chest X-ray is enough vs CT, minimum visits, and cheaper medication options. If the clinician is empathic, respond appreciatively and become more forthcoming.

medications: lisinopril 10 mg PO once daily
albuterol HFA 90 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled every 4-6 hours as needed for wheeze/shortness of breath
confounders: subacute infectious pneumonia treated with prior outpatient antibiotics without clear improvement
GERD with possible microaspiration contributing to chronic cough and patchy infiltrates
ACE-inhibitor (lisinopril) associated cough potentially masking pulmonary symptom progression
recent exposure to household mold/damp basement (hypersensitivity pneumonitis in differential)
former smoker (15 pack-years) increasing concern for malignancy-associated organizing pneumonia

acceptable_alternatives: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (bronchiolitis obliterans organizing pneumonia, BOOP)
required_evidence: Subacute respiratory illness (days to weeks) with persistent symptoms despite typical antibiotics or without clear infectious source
Focal bronchial breath sounds consistent with consolidation-like process
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP and/or ESR) with variable WBC
No alternative explanation on initial evaluation (e.g., no clear heart failure, pulmonary embolism, or confirmed bacterial pneumonia)

Need for a gradual taper over months to reduce relapse risk
Key steroid adverse effects to monitor (hyperglycemia, mood changes, insomnia, GI upset, infection risk)
Discuss red flags requiring urgent care (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, hemoptysis, low oxygen symptoms)
Discuss possibility of relapse and importance of follow-up

Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds for corticosteroid interactions (e.g., warfarin, diabetes meds, NSAIDs, strong CYP3A4 inhibitors) and plan monitoring/adjustments.
Start systemic corticosteroid therapy (first-line) with an appropriate plan for taper over months; include monitoring plan for adverse effects and consider GI/bone protection when indicated.

Meet all communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment and adverse effects, address concerns/red flags/relapse risk, and provide lifestyle and infection-prevention guidance while on steroids.
Arrange follow-up to assess response within days to 1–2 weeks, and longer-term follow-up with repeat imaging and taper adjustments; ensure clear return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and ensuring safety-netting and follow-up are in place.

Critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with current meds/supplements/substances (e.g., QT-prolongers, CYP inhibitors/inducers, sedatives).

Elicit patient/family goals and concerns (school, stigma, safety, medication fears), preferences (sedation vs activation, weight concerns), and feasibility of adherence; discuss formulation options (oral vs LAI where appropriate).

Provide lifestyle and supports education: avoid alcohol/cannabis/illicit drugs; sleep hygiene; regular meals/activity; family routines; therapy and school accommodations; crisis plan (worsening symptoms, suicidality/violence) and when to use urgent/ER services.

Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm no contraindications/major precautions for selected antipsychotic (e.g., pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, QTc prolongation/arrhythmia risk, severe hepatic impairment, history of NMS, uncontrolled seizures, severe hypotension).

Arrange close follow-up (typically within 1 week after initiation/titration) and ongoing monitoring; include plan for early reassessment of response, side effects, adherence, and safety.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, return precautions, and follow-up plan.

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Normal to mildly elevated (often 4-12 x10^9/L); may be normal early
expected_lab_results.CBC - Neutrophils: Normal to mildly increased (relative neutrophilia can occur early in infection)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Lymphocytes: Normal to mildly decreased early; may normalize later (no marked lymphocytosis expected on CBC alone)
expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Often normal; may be mildly low (mild thrombocytopenia possible but not a defining feature)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (mild hyponatremia possible, including SIADH in CNS infections)
expected_lab_results.CMP - Chloride: Typically normal or mildly low if hyponatremic

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old woman, cooperative and clear. Start by saying: “I’ve been having this burning when I pee, and I’m running to the bathroom all the time.” Volunteer: burning with urination, frequent urge with little output, stronger-smelling urine. If asked, add: started 3 days ago and worsening, low belly pressure/discomfort, urine a little cloudy, no fever noticed, no back/flank pain. Do not volunteer at first: you’ve been holding urine at work due to few breaks, drinking little water, sometimes wiping back-to-front when rushed, and you used a new bubble bath/soapy product and felt irritation after—share only if asked about hygiene, triggers, hydration, or work habits. tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for suspected appendicitis (pain onset/migration, anorexia, nausea/vomiting, fever), red flags, pregnancy status if relevant, and baseline risk factors.
Order initial labs to support diagnosis and pre-op status (e.g., CBC, CMP/electrolytes/renal function, CRP if available, urinalysis; pregnancy test when applicable).
Review results to assess inflammatory markers, renal function for medication choices, and alternate diagnoses (e.g., UA).
Commit to working diagnosis of appendicitis based on clinical picture and available data; acknowledge imaging/exam/trajectory guide decisions.
Perform critical safety check before prescribing; clarify penicillin allergy phenotype (e.g., urticaria/angioedema/anaphylaxis vs intolerance) to guide antibiotic selection.
Non-critical safety check prior to prescribing to avoid harmful interactions (especially if considering fluoroquinolone, metronidazole, opioids/antiemetics).
Initiate appropriate appendicitis management orders: NPO, IV fluids, analgesia/antiemetic, and empiric antibiotics consistent with allergy status and renal function; avoid contraindicated options.

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (urgent surgery, NPO/IVF/antibiotics, symptom control), address allergy/side effects/return precautions, and lifestyle/post-op expectations.
Ensure appropriate disposition planning: urgent surgical evaluation/transfer; post-op follow-up instructions if applicable.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and ensuring urgent escalation pathway is in place.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent bringing your 9-year-old son. Act anxious and vague, worried it’s getting worse fast. Start with: “After a sore throat about 3 weeks ago, he suddenly developed really bad weird habits and nonstop worries.” Volunteer: sudden repetitive handwashing, constant reassurance-seeking (“Are you sure I didn’t touch something dirty?”), big mood swings/meltdowns, trouble sleeping and needing you in the room. Do not mention unless directly asked: throat-clearing/sniffing tics, revved-up then tearful episodes, getting stuck on routines, weight loss, and a similar smaller episode after an infection last year. Be hesitant/embarrassed about: “bad thoughts,” ‘just-right’ bedtime rituals, genital checking, and one comment about wishing he could disappear—only disclose if asked about safety or severe thoughts. confounders: Primary OCD/tic disorder (Tourette spectrum) independent of infection trigger
Recent psychosocial stressor (school transition) temporally associated with symptom onset
Stimulant-associated anxiety/irritability that can mimic or worsen obsessive-compulsive symptoms
Negative throat culture/rapid strep despite elevated ASO/anti-DNase B indicating prior (not necessarily current) GAS exposure

correct_diagnosis.primary: Pediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorders associated with streptococcal infections (PANDAS)
required_evidence: Abrupt, dramatic onset of OCD symptoms and/or motor/vocal tics in a prepubertal child
Episodic course with symptom exacerbations
Temporal association with Group A Streptococcus infection (positive throat culture/rapid antigen or rising ASO titer)
Neurologic abnormalities during exacerbation (e.g., motor hyperactivity, choreiform movements, tics) and/or severe anxiety/emotional lability
Exclusion of alternative neurologic/medical causes explaining symptoms (e.g., Sydenham chorea/ARF, encephalitis)

expected_lab_results.Rapid strep antigen: May be positive during/near symptom onset; if negative does not exclude recent infection
expected_lab_results.Throat culture (Group A Strep): May be positive; if negative can still have evidence of recent infection via serology
expected_lab_results.Antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer: Elevated and/or rising on repeat testing (paired titers 2–4 weeks apart) consistent with recent GAS exposure
expected_lab_results.CRP: Normal to mildly elevated (often normal in isolated PANDAS)
expected_lab_results.CBC WBC: Often normal; may show mild leukocytosis if concurrent infection

medications: Azithromycin 10 mg/kg PO once daily on day 1 (max 500 mg), then 5 mg/kg PO once daily on days 2-5 (max 250 mg) for suspected/confirmed GAS pharyngitis in penicillin allergy
Ibuprofen 10 mg/kg PO every 6-8 hours as needed for pain/fever (max 40 mg/kg/day)
Sertraline 12.5-25 mg PO once daily for OCD/anxiety (start low; titrate every 1-2 weeks as tolerated/needed)
Melatonin 1-3 mg PO nightly as needed for sleep onset insomnia
non_pharmacological: Confirm GAS infection with throat testing; obtain paired ASO titers if initial testing negative but clinical suspicion remains
Cognitive behavioral therapy with exposure and response prevention (CBT-ERP) as first-line for OCD symptoms
Family education: symptom tracking, relapse plan, school accommodations during exacerbations
Assess severity and safety: screen for suicidality, self-harm, severe restriction, dehydration, inability to attend school; urgent referral if present

Consider referral to pediatric neurology/child psychiatry; consider immunomodulatory therapy (e.g., corticosteroids/IVIG) only in severe cases and typically in specialist care after diagnostic confirmation
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 1-2 weeks to review test results, response to antibiotics/supportive care, and psychiatric symptom trajectory; sooner (24-72 hours) if rapid worsening or safety concerns

must_include: Definition: sudden onset OCD/tics temporally linked to recent strep infection; immune-mediated hypothesis
Diagnosis is clinical and requires ruling out other causes; tests support but do not perfectly confirm
Course can be episodic with flares; not the same as typical gradual-onset OCD/tic disorders

Core symptom treatment includes CBT-ERP and, when needed, cautious SSRI use with slow titration

Review red flags requiring urgent care: suicidality, severe aggression, inability to eat/drink, severe functional decline, neurologic red flags (true chorea, weakness, altered mental status)
Discuss uncertainty/controversy: evidence base varies; focus on treating infection and symptoms while monitoring
Shared decision-making on antibiotics, therapy, and specialty referrals
Infection prevention: hand hygiene; evaluate symptomatic household contacts per local guidance
School plan: temporary accommodations, reduced triggers, structured routine during flares
Symptom diary: track OCD/tics, sleep, infections, and response to treatments to guide follow-up

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for abrupt onset OCD/tics, temporal link to recent GAS infection, associated neuropsychiatric symptoms, functional impairment, and red flags; clarify prior strep testing/treatment and medication history.

Order tests to support/assess recent or active GAS infection and evaluate alternative explanations when indicated (e.g., throat swab/NAAT or rapid antigen with backup culture; consider ASO/anti-DNase B if timing suggests).
Review GAS testing results to guide antibiotic decision-making and document whether infection is active/suspected.
Provide a clinical working diagnosis (PANDAS vs PANS vs primary OCD/tic disorder) and explicitly note the need to rule out other causes and that tests are supportive but not definitive.
Critical safety check before prescribing: confirm allergy history (urticarial rash with amoxicillin) and characterize reaction severity to guide antibiotic selection.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing: screen for interactions with planned antibiotics and any psychotropic medications (e.g., SSRIs) or OTC agents.
If active/suspected GAS infection, prescribe an appropriate non-penicillin antibiotic regimen given amoxicillin urticarial rash; avoid contraindicated options; include dosing duration and counseling.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment plan (CBT-ERP; cautious SSRI with slow titration when needed), expectations, red flags, uncertainty/controversy, infection prevention, school accommodations, and symptom diary.
Arrange timely follow-up to reassess symptoms, treatment response, side effects, and need for referrals (pediatrics, child psychiatry, neurology, infectious disease as appropriate).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explicitly elicits abrupt onset of OCD and/or tics with temporal association to a recent sore throat/fever or documented GAS exposure/infection, including timing (days–weeks) and episodic flares.

Agent screens for alternative diagnoses and red flags: suicidality, severe aggression, inability to eat/drink/dehydration, severe functional decline/catatonia, altered mental status, focal neurologic deficits/weakness, true chorea, seizures, or signs of CNS infection.

Agent states PANDAS is a clinical diagnosis requiring exclusion of other causes; testing (throat testing, ASO/anti-DNase B) can support but does not perfectly confirm; course can be episodic and differs from typical gradual-onset OCD/tic disorders.

If prescribing antibiotics, agent performs allergy_check before prescribing and uses the documented amoxicillin urticarial rash to select a non-penicillin option; agent avoids recommending amoxicillin/penicillin and avoids cephalosporins if the scenario indicates immediate-type beta-lactam allergy without discussing risk/alternatives.

Agent performs contraindication_check before prescribing (critical): confirms age/weight appropriateness, pregnancy status if relevant, hepatic/renal disease, QT-risk history if selecting macrolides, and prior adverse reactions.

Agent performs drug_interaction_check before prescribing (non-critical): checks interactions between antibiotics (e.g., macrolides) and SSRIs/other meds; does not prescribe interacting combinations without mitigation/monitoring.

Agent includes core symptom treatment: recommends CBT with ERP as first-line for OCD; if SSRIs are used, emphasizes cautious use with slow titration and monitoring for activation, irritability, suicidality, or worsening behavior.
Agent sets expectations that psychiatric symptoms may take weeks to improve even after infection treatment; provides monitoring plan and safety-net instructions.
Agent uses shared decision-making on antibiotics, therapy, and specialty referrals; documents family preferences and discusses risks/benefits and uncertainty.

Agent schedules follow-up within an appropriate timeframe (e.g., 1–2 weeks after starting antibiotics or sooner if severe symptoms) and provides urgent return precautions.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit HAE-relevant history and acuity: swelling location (face/lips/tongue/throat), voice change/stridor/dyspnea, abdominal pain/vomiting, onset/duration, presence/absence of urticaria/pruritus, prior similar episodes, triggers (trauma, stress, infection), medication triggers (ACE-I, estrogen), family history, prior treatments/response, pregnancy status, and current severity to determine need for emergent airway management.

Order diagnostic labs to support HAE and distinguish from allergic/anaphylaxis and acquired angioedema: C4, C1 inhibitor antigenic level, C1 inhibitor functional assay; add C1q (acquired vs hereditary) and serum tryptase (mast-cell mediated/anaphylaxis).
Interpret lab pattern (e.g., low C4 with low/abnormal C1-INH; normal C1q in hereditary; elevated tryptase suggests allergic/anaphylaxis) to confirm/strengthen diagnosis and guide counseling.
Document likely diagnosis (hereditary angioedema) and explicitly classify as bradykinin-mediated angioedema when urticaria/pruritus absent and labs support C1-INH deficiency/dysfunction.

Critical safety check before prescribing: verify allergy history (including penicillin allergy details) and confirm no known allergy to proposed on-demand HAE therapies (plasma-derived/recombinant C1-INH, icatibant, ecallantide) or excipients; clarify prior reactions and severity.

Non-critical safety check before prescribing: review for potential interactions and medication triggers (ACE inhibitors, estrogen-containing therapies, DPP-4 inhibitors) and ensure proposed therapy is appropriate with current medications.

Initiate evidence-based on-demand therapy for acute HAE attack (C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide) with dosing/route and clear instructions; include urgency/ED referral criteria for airway symptoms; avoid relying on antihistamines/steroids/epinephrine unless true anaphylaxis suspected.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (bradykinin-mediated, C1-INH/C4, role of C1q/tryptase, dangerous sites), explain treatment expectations and emergency thresholds, address concerns (allergies, adverse effects, recurrence plan, family screening), and lifestyle/prophylaxis guidance (avoid ACE-I/estrogen, procedure prophylaxis, action plan/medical alert, keep rescue meds available).

Arrange specialist follow-up (allergy/immunology) for confirmation, long-term management (on-demand supply, consideration of prophylaxis), family testing, and procedure planning; provide timeframe and contingency if symptoms worsen.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-net instructions and follow-up plan are in place.

must_include: This is a chronic inflammatory arthritis beginning in childhood; LACC1-related disease can behave like juvenile idiopathic arthritis
Goals are to control inflammation, prevent joint damage, and maintain normal growth/function
Explain what labs (ESR/CRP/CBC/ferritin) are used for: tracking inflammation and medication safety
Sulfasalazine is a disease-modifying medicine; it can take several weeks to improve symptoms
How to take it (divided doses, with food if GI upset) and importance of adherence
Monitoring plan: periodic CBC and liver tests (and clinical monitoring for rash, fever, sore throat, bruising/bleeding) and when to stop/seek care

Red flags requiring urgent evaluation: high fever, severe lethargy, new rash/mouth sores, jaundice/dark urine, shortness of breath, severe abdominal pain, signs of infection

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 52-year-old male warehouse supervisor. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by volunteering: progressive leg weakness with frequent tripping, worsening over time; stairs are hard and you pull on the railing; rising from low chair/toilet is difficult; grip is weaker (jars, grocery bags); muscles feel tired/shaky after small tasks even without feeling out of breath.

medications: Atorvastatin 20 mg nightly
Lisinopril 10 mg once daily
Metformin ER 500 mg once daily with evening meal
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU once daily
confounders: Statin-associated myopathy can mimic or worsen proximal muscle weakness and elevate CK
Vitamin D deficiency can contribute to myalgias and weakness
Prediabetes with possible early peripheral neuropathy can complicate assessment of gait instability
Mildly elevated AST from skeletal muscle injury may be misattributed to liver disease
Possible early cardiomyopathy/arrhythmia risk in myofibrillar myopathy (suggested by elevated NT-proBNP) can complicate exertional dyspnea evaluation

acceptable_alternatives: Inherited myopathy (suspected myofibrillar myopathy pending genetic confirmation)
Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy (LGMD) as a differential diagnosis to be ruled out
Inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis/dermatomyositis) to be ruled out
required_evidence: Progressive muscle weakness (often proximal and/or distal) with chronic course and functional decline
Myopathic pattern on EMG (if obtained) and/or muscle MRI showing selective muscle involvement
Muscle biopsy consistent with myofibrillar myopathy: myofibrillar disorganization with protein aggregates (e.g., desmin-positive inclusions), rimmed vacuoles, Z-disc abnormalities
Genetic testing identifying a pathogenic variant in an MFM-associated gene (e.g., DES, FLNC, MYOT, CRYAB, BAG3, LDB3/ZASP)
Exclusion of common acquired causes of myopathy (thyroid disease, uncontrolled diabetes, vitamin D deficiency as contributor, medication/toxin-induced myopathy)

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (often <1000 IU/L); may be intermittently elevated
expected_lab_results.AST: Normal or mildly elevated (can reflect muscle source rather than liver if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal or mildly elevated (can reflect muscle source rather than liver if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Typically normal (used to exclude hypothyroid myopathy)
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: May be normal or elevated depending on comorbid diabetes; not diagnostic for MFM
expected_lab_results.Vitamin D (25-OH): May be low; deficiency can worsen myalgias/weakness but does not explain progressive myopathy alone

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily (if 25-OH vitamin D is low)
non_pharmacological: Refer to Neuromuscular specialist for confirmation (genetic testing, consideration of muscle biopsy if not already done) and longitudinal management
Physical therapy focusing on low-to-moderate intensity strengthening, stretching, balance training; avoid overexertion and eccentric overloading that provokes prolonged soreness/weakness
Occupational therapy for energy conservation, adaptive equipment, and fall-prevention strategies
Baseline and periodic cardiac evaluation (ECG and echocardiogram) given risk of cardiomyopathy/conduction disease in some MFM genotypes; consider Holter monitoring if palpitations/syncope
Pulmonary function testing (including FVC sitting/supine) and sleep assessment if symptoms of hypoventilation or sleep-disordered breathing; consider NIV if indicated
Assistive devices as needed (cane/walker/AFO) and home safety evaluation to reduce falls
Genetic counseling for patient and family; discuss inheritance patterns and cascade testing where appropriate
Review and minimize myotoxic exposures (e.g., unnecessary statins, excessive alcohol) and ensure vaccinations/respiratory infection prevention
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 4-8 weeks (or sooner if rapid decline); PT/OT initiation within 1-2 weeks; cardiac/pulmonary screening within 4-12 weeks depending on symptoms

must_include: This is a chronic muscle disease where muscle fibers develop abnormal protein clumps/structural disruption, leading to progressive weakness
Course is variable; symptoms can slowly worsen over years and may affect walking, hand function, and sometimes breathing or heart
Diagnosis is confirmed with genetic testing and/or muscle biopsy; blood tests alone are not definitive
No curative medication currently; treatment focuses on maintaining function, preventing complications, and symptom management
PT/OT and appropriate exercise are beneficial, but overexertion can worsen symptoms—plan should be individualized
Need for heart and breathing screening because some genetic subtypes can involve these organs
Discuss prognosis uncertainty and individualized expectations (functional goals, mobility aids when needed)
Discuss genetic implications for family members and offer genetic counseling

Review red flags requiring urgent evaluation: new chest pain, palpitations/syncope, rapidly worsening weakness, new shortness of breath, morning headaches or daytime sleepiness suggesting hypoventilation
Fall prevention strategies and home safety modifications
Energy conservation and pacing; avoid prolonged eccentric or high-intensity workouts that trigger prolonged weakness
Optimize general health: adequate protein intake, maintain healthy weight, manage diabetes if present, treat vitamin D deficiency if found

Order baseline evaluation to support myopathy workup and exclude mimics/complications (e.g., CK, CMP/LFTs, TSH, HbA1c/glucose, vitamin D, B12, CBC, ESR/CRP as appropriate).

Order organ involvement screening if clinically indicated/available: ECG and echocardiogram; pulmonary function tests (FVC sitting/supine) ± nocturnal oximetry/capnography if symptoms suggest hypoventilation.
Review ordered results to assess severity, exclude alternative etiologies, and identify treatable contributors (e.g., vitamin D deficiency, diabetes).
State likely diagnosis and confirmatory plan: emphasize confirmation via genetic testing and/or muscle biopsy; labs alone are not definitive.
Explain diagnosis, expected course variability, and key complications; provide treatment approach focusing on function, PT/OT, individualized exercise, and need for heart/breathing screening.

Address patient concerns: prognosis uncertainty, functional goals, mobility aids, genetic implications and genetic counseling, and urgent red flags (cardiac and respiratory warning symptoms, rapid decline).

Lifestyle counseling: fall prevention/home safety, pacing/energy conservation, avoid prolonged eccentric/high-intensity exercise causing prolonged weakness, optimize general health (protein, weight, diabetes control, treat vitamin D deficiency if found).
Required before any prescribing to prevent allergic reactions (critical).
Check for drug-drug interactions before prescribing (non-critical but expected if prescribing).

If prescribing symptomatic/supportive meds or supplements, ensure allergy and interaction checks are completed and avoid contraindicated agents; focus on appropriate therapies (e.g., vitamin D repletion if deficient, pain management if needed).
Arrange follow-up for results review, referrals (neuromuscular specialist, genetics, PT/OT), and monitoring of respiratory/cardiac status.
Close the encounter after plan, safety-netting, and follow-up are established.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a cooperative 19-year-old college student/barista. Speak clearly and calmly, a bit worried but not dramatic. If asked about other bleeding, disclose: small cuts ooze longer, prolonged bleeding after dental work (had to return), bleeding that “reopens” the next day, very heavy long periods, and feeling more tired/run down. Do not mention unless specifically asked: painful large bruises/lumps after shots/bumps, avoiding sports due to fear of bleeding/bruising, and shortness of breath on stairs especially around periods. Express misconceptions: because joints don’t swell/hurt, you thought it couldn’t be hemophilia; you think factor/blood products are too expensive and should be saved for emergencies; you assume hematology visits/labs are optional due to cost.
